cards.grammarNote: يَجِبُ أَنْ (yajib an) = it is necessary that / must. The verb following أَنْ is in the subjunctive (المضارع المنصوب). The subjunctive of تَسْتَرِيحُ drops the final -u to give تَسْتَرِيحَ.

آخُذَ (ākhudha) is the subjunctive of آخُذُ (I take). Note the subjunctive ending -a instead of -u. دَوَاءً is indefinite accusative (tanwin -an). يَجِبُ is impersonal — it doesn't change for person/gender.

cards.front: You should go to the doctor.
cards.grammarNote: لَازِمٌ (lāzim) = necessary/must. It is common in spoken Arabic and MSA. Like يَجِبُ, it can be followed by أَنْ + subjunctive, or in colloquial usage directly by the present verb as shown here.
يَنَامَ is the subjunctive of يَنَامُ (he sleeps). The subjunctive is triggered by أَنْ after modal verbs. The verb agrees with its logical subject: يَنَامَ (he), تَنَامَ (she), أَنَامَ (I).
يَنْبَغِي (yanbaghī) = should/ought to — more formal than لَازِم. Negation: يَنْبَغِي أَلَّا (yanbaghī allā) = it should not that. أَلَّا = أَنْ + لَا contracted together.

cards.front: You must see the doctor immediately.

cards.grammarNote: مِنَ الضَّرُورِيّ أَنْ (min al-ḍarūriyy an) = it is necessary to. Another way to express strong obligation, slightly more formal. ضَرُورِيّ = necessary/essential.

cards.front: I must take the pills twice a day.
cards.back: يَجِبُ أَنْ آخُذَ الْحُبُوبَ مَرَّتَيْنِ فِي الْيَوْم.
cards.romanized: yajib an ākhudha l-ḥubūba marratayni fī l-yawm.
cards.grammarNote: مَرَّتَيْنِ (marratayni) = twice (lit. 'two times'), the dual of مَرَّة (marra = once). الْحُبُوبَ (al-ḥubūba) = the pills, accusative plural of حَبَّة (ḥabba = pill/grain).
